import polars as pl
import polars.selectors as cs
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"

title: Can You Validate Me?
text:
Data Loading & Cleaning
The data cleaning mirrors the approach from Task 3 — the two-row header is merged into a single set of column names before the data rows are read in.


Show the code
a = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=10, n_rows=1, has_header=False,
                encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

a[2] = "seen_any"

a = (
    a.str.replace(r".+seen.+",       "seen_")
     .str.replace(r".+rank.+",       "rank_")
     .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
     .fill_null(strategy="forward")
     .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "")
     .str.strip_chars()
     .str.to_lowercase()
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")
)

a[3]  = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"

b = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=11, n_rows=1, has_header=False,
                encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

b = (
    b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
     .str.replace("Response", "")
     .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
     .fill_null("")
     .str.strip_chars()
)

new_names = (a + b).to_list()

df = pl.read_csv(
    data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False,
    new_columns=new_names
)

print(f"Rows: {len(df)}  |  Columns: {len(df.columns)}")


Rows: 1186  |  Columns: 39

section: Chart 1 — “Which ‘Star Wars’ Films Have You Seen?”
text:
Chart 1 — “Which ‘Star Wars’ Films Have You Seen?”


Show the code
fans = df.filter(pl.col("seen_any") == "Yes")
n_fans = len(fans)

seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]

episode_labels = {
    "seen_Episode_I":   "The Phantom Menace",
    "seen_Episode_II":  "Attack of the Clones",
    "seen_Episode_III": "Revenge of the Sith",
    "seen_Episode_IV":  "A New Hope",
    "seen_Episode_V":   "The Empire Strikes Back",
    "seen_Episode_VI":  "Return of the Jedi",
}

seen_counts = pl.DataFrame({
    "col":   seen_cols,
    "n_seen": [fans[c].drop_nulls().len() for c in seen_cols],
}).with_columns(
    (pl.col("n_seen") / n_fans * 100).alias("pct"),
    pl.col("col").replace(episode_labels).alias("film"),
).sort("pct", descending=True).with_columns(
    (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int32).cast(pl.String) + "%").alias("label")
)

print(seen_counts.select(["film", "pct"]))

title: Can You Validate Me?
text:
Validation

Chart 1 — Films Seen
Pretty much what you’d expect, the original trilogy (Episodes IV–VI) was seen by around 79–83% of fans, and the prequels drop off to somewhere in the 53–60% range. Our chart looks basically the same as the one in the article, so the data checks out here.


Chart 2 — Best Film
The Empire Strikes Back is the most popular, grabbing about 36% of #1 votes, with A New Hope not too far behind at around 25%. The prequels are basically a rounding error. That matches the article pretty closely, so the ranking data looks good too.


Overall
Both charts line up well enough with the article that we can trust the dataset. The numbers aren’t going to be pixel-perfect since these are survey estimates, but the story they tell is the same.

section: Question 1: Filter to Respondents Who Have Seen at Least One Film
text:
Question 1: Filter to Respondents Who Have Seen at Least One Film
So the instructions specifically say NOT to use the seen_any column even though that sounds like exactly what you’d want. Instead, I’m looking at the six individual per-movie columns, one for each episode. If any of those are filled in, the person has seen at least one film and they stay in. If all six are null, they skipped the whole section which probably means they haven’t seen any, so they get dropped.


Show the code
# The six per-episode "have you seen this film" columns
seen_cols = [f"seen_Episode_{ep}" for ep in ["I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"]]

# Keep rows where at least one episode column is not null
df_seen = df.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal([pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in seen_cols])
)

print(f"Filtered dataset — Rows: {df_seen.height}  (expected: 835)")

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey (Task 2)
section: Question 2: Recode Range Variables for Machine Learning
text:
Question 2: Recode Range Variables for Machine Learning
This is the main thing this task is about. Machine learning models can’t do anything with strings like "30-44" or "$50,000 - $99,999". Those need to become actual numbers. So I’m going through each of the problem columns and converting them. For ranges I’m using the midpoint of the range as the number (so "18-29" becomes 23.5), and for ordered categories like education or favorability I’m using ordinal encoding where each level gets a number in order. After making the new numeric column I delete the old string one since we don’t need it anymore.


2a: Age
There are only four age buckets so this one’s pretty simple. I just picked the midpoint for each range. The last one "> 60" is open-ended so there’s no real midpoint, so I went with 65 which seemed like a fair middle-of-the-road guess for that group.


Show the code
age_map = {
    "18-29": 23.5,
    "30-44": 37.0,
    "45-60": 52.5,
    "> 60":  65.0,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("age")
      .replace(age_map, return_dtype=pl.Float64)
      .alias("age_num")
).drop("age")

print("age_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["age_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


age_num unique values: [23.5, 37.0, 52.5, 65.0]





2b: Education
Education is a natural ladder from least to most schooling, so I’m just numbering the levels 1 through 5. No diploma at the bottom gets a 1, grad degree at the top gets a 5. Simple enough.


Show the code
edu_map = {
    "Less than high school degree":       1,
    "High school degree":                 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree":   3,
    "Bachelor degree":                    4,
    "Graduate degree":                    5,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("education")
      .replace(edu_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("education_num")
).drop("education")

print("education_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["education_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


education_num unique values: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]





2c: Household Income
Same idea as age, just use the midpoint of each bracket. The tricky one is "$150,000+" since it has no upper end. I went with $175,000 as a rough estimate. It’s not perfect but it’s better than leaving it as a string or just using 150,000 which would underrepresent that group.


Show the code
income_map = {
    "$0 - $24,999":          12_500,
    "$25,000 - $49,999":     37_500,
    "$50,000 - $99,999":     75_000,
    "$100,000 - $149,999":  125_000,
    "$150,000+":            175_000,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("household_income")
      .replace(income_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("income_num")
).drop("household_income")

print("income_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["income_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


income_num unique values: [12500, 37500, 75000, 125000, 175000]





2d: Favorability Ratings
This one was actually kind of satisfying to figure out. There are 14 favorability columns (one per character), and they ALL use the exact same 6-level scale, so I don’t have to write the same map 14 times. I can just loop over all the columns that start with fav_ and apply the same replacement to each one in a single pass.
I set "Unfamiliar (N/A)" to 0 instead of null because “I don’t know who this is” is meaningfully different from hating someone. It seemed wrong to just drop those responses.


Show the code
fav_map = {
    "Unfamiliar (N/A)":                             0,
    "Very unfavorably":                             1,
    "Somewhat unfavorably":                         2,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)":  3,
    "Somewhat favorably":                           4,
    "Very favorably":                               5,
}

fav_cols = [c for c in df_seen.columns if c.startswith("fav_")]

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns([
    pl.col(c).replace(fav_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32).alias(c + "_num")
    for c in fav_cols
]).drop(fav_cols)

print(f"Replaced {len(fav_cols)} fav_ columns.")
print("Sample fav_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["fav_Han_Solo_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


Replaced 14 fav_ columns.
Sample fav_num unique values: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
import polars.selectors as cs
# add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"



# Read in and tidy up the first row
a = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))
# Note the non-aschii character in the column "Do you consider yourself to be a fan of the Expanded Universe". (column AF if you open it in Excel). 

# BTW: Detecting what it is and where can be difficult

# Lossy means that invalid utf8 values are replaced with <EFBFBD> characters. When using other encodings than utf8 or utf8-lossy, the input is first decoded in memory with python. Defaults to utf8...ignore_errors = True does not replace the non-utf8 character with a blank space like you would hope

a[2] = "seen_any" #simply overwrite the value at index 2

# Rather than changing each column header manually,
# here we use regex to apply changes more efficiently
a = (
  a.str.replace(r".+seen.+", "seen_")
   .str.replace(r".+rank.+", "rank_")
   .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
   .fill_null(strategy = "forward")

   .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "") #this gets rid of the question marks in a diamond characters 
   .str.strip_chars()
   .str.to_lowercase()
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")

)
a[3] = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"



b = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 11, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))

b = (
  b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
   .str.replace("Response", "")
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .fill_null("")
   .str.strip_chars()
)


#Now combine the two series, each of which contains part of the desired column header
new_names = (a + b).to_list()

# Read in the data, skipping over the column header rows
# and use 'new_names' as the column headers
df = pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False, new_columns = new_names )

title: Client Report - Show me More!
text:
STRETCH QUESTION 1
Join the dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv data to dwellings_ml.csv on the parcel column and re-run the recommended model (Random Forest). Explain the differences and if this changes the model you recommend to the Client.
The original dataset only knows stuff about the house itself, like how big it is or what it’s made of. The neighborhood dataset adds info about the area around the house. The idea is that some parts of Denver were mostly built up before 1980, so knowing which neighborhood a house is in might help the model make a better guess.


Show the code
# Deduplicate neighborhoods so each parcel appears once, then join
neighborhoods_dedup = neighborhoods.unique(subset=["parcel"], keep="first")
combined = dwellings.join(neighborhoods_dedup, on="parcel", how="left")

# Feature sets (exclude non-feature columns)
exclude = {"parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980"}

features_base = [c for c in dwellings.columns   if c not in exclude]
features_comb = [c for c in combined.columns    if c not in exclude]

# Cast any remaining string columns to numeric (one-hot already done in source data)
def to_numpy_features(df, feature_list):
    return df.select(feature_list).fill_null(0).to_numpy()

X_base = to_numpy_features(dwellings, features_base)
X_comb = to_numpy_features(combined,  features_comb)
y       = dwellings["before1980"].to_numpy()

# Use the same random split for fair comparison
X_base_train, X_base_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_base, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)
X_comb_train, X_comb_test, _, _ = train_test_split(
    X_comb, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)

# Train identical Random Forest configs on both datasets
rf_base = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
rf_base.fit(X_base_train, y_train)
y_pred_base = rf_base.predict(X_base_test)

y_prob_comb = rf_comb.predict_proba(X_comb_test)[:, 1]

for label, y_pred, y_prob in [
    ("Baseline (no neighborhoods)", y_pred_base, y_prob_base),
    ("With neighborhoods joined",   y_pred_comb, y_prob_comb),
]:
    acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    auc = metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    print(f"{label}")
    print(f"  Accuracy : {acc:.4f}")
    print(f"  ROC-AUC  : {auc:.4f}\n")

title: Client Report - Show me More!
text:
STRETCH QUESTION 2
Build a regression model that predicts the year a house was built. Report appropriate evaluation metrics.
Up until now we’ve only asked the model a yes/no question, such as, was it built before 1980? Now im going to ask it to guess the actual year. This is a regression problem instead of a classification problem, so I switch over to a Random Forest Regressor. Its the same general idea (a bunch of decision trees working together), but instead of outputting “yes” or “no” it spits out a number like 1974.


Show the code
# Use the baseline feature set (no neighborhoods) for simplicity
features_reg = [c for c in dwellings.columns if c not in {"parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980"}]

X_reg = dwellings.select(features_reg).to_numpy()
y_reg = dwellings["yrbuilt"].to_numpy()

X_reg_train, X_reg_test, y_reg_train, y_reg_test = train_test_split(
    X_reg, y_reg, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)

rf_reg = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1)
rf_reg.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)
y_reg_pred = rf_reg.predict(X_reg_test)




Show the code
# Regression evaluation metrics
mae  = metrics.mean_absolute_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(metrics.mean_squared_error(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred))
r2   = metrics.r2_score(y_reg_test, y_reg_pred)

print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE) : {mae:.2f} years")
print(f"Root Mean Squared Error   : {rmse:.2f} years")
print(f"R²                        : {r2:.4f}")


Mean Absolute Error (MAE) : 10.38 years
Root Mean Squared Error   : 16.90 years
R²                        : 0.7887




Show the code
# Actual vs Predicted scatter plot
sample_idx = np.random.default_rng(42).choice(len(y_reg_test), size=500, replace=False)
actual_s    = y_reg_test[sample_idx].tolist()
predicted_s = y_reg_pred[sample_idx].tolist()

chart_scatter = (
    ggplot({"actual": actual_s, "predicted": predicted_s}, aes(x="actual", y="predicted"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.4, color="#2171b5", size=2)
    + geom_abline(slope=1, intercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed")
    + labs(
        x="Actual Year Built",
        y="Predicted Year Built",
        title="Regression: Actual vs. Predicted Year Built (500-row sample)"
    )
)
chart_scatter.show()



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   





Show the code
# Residual distribution
residuals = (y_reg_test - y_reg_pred).tolist()

chart_resid = (
    ggplot({"residual": residuals}, aes(x="residual"))
    + geom_histogram(bins=50, fill="#2171b5", color="white")
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="red", linetype="dashed")
    + labs(
        x="Residual (Actual − Predicted)",
        y="Count",
        title="Residual Distribution — Year Built Regression"
    )
)
chart_resid.show()



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   





Show the code
# Top feature importances for the regression model
perm_reg = permutation_importance(rf_reg, X_reg_test, y_reg_test, n_repeats=5, random_state=42, n_jobs=1)
imp_reg_df = (
    pl.DataFrame({"feature": features_reg, "importance": perm_reg.importances_mean.tolist()})
    .sort("importance", descending=True)
    .head(10)
)
chart_imp = (
    ggplot(
        {"feature": imp_reg_df["feature"].to_list(), "importance": imp_reg_df["importance"].to_list()},
        aes(x="feature", y="importance")
    )
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#238b45")
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        x="Feature",
        y="Mean Increase in MSE",
        title="Top 10 Permutation Importances — Year Built Regressor"
    )
)
chart_imp.show()



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   



What the numbers mean:

MAE (Mean Absolute Error) is probably the easiest one to understand since it’s just the average number of years the model was off. So if MAE is 8, the model’s guesses were off by about 8 years on average. That’s not bad when you think about how hard it is to guess a year just from square footage and style.
RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) is similar to MAE but it punishes big mistakes extra. If RMSE is way higher than MAE, it means a handful of predictions were really far off like guessing 2000 for a house actually built in 1955. That can happen when an old house got heavily remodeled.
R² is basically “how much of the variation in year built does the model actually explain?” An R² of 0.80 means the model accounts for 80% of why years differ between houses. Higher is better, if it’s below 0.5 the model is just guessing.
The scatter plot shows actual year on the x-axis and what the model predicted on the y-axis. If it were a perfect model, every dot would be right on the red dashed line. The closer the dots are to that line, the better.
The residual histogram shows how wrong the model was for each house. If it’s roughly centered on zero, that means the model isn’t consistently guessing too high or too low and it’s just normally wrong in both directions, which is what you want.

Honestly, for what the client actually needs (figuring out which houses might have asbestos), the yes/no classifier from the last task is more useful. Knowing a house was “probably built around 1972” is cool, but knowing “it was almost definitely built before 1980” is more actionable and useful in my opinion. The regression model is a nice bonus but I wouldn’t swap out the classifier for it.

QUESTION 1

Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics. You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.

I used three different ways to check how well my model is doing:

Accuracy – This is just the percentage of homes the model guessed right. My model got about 93%, which means it only got about 7 out of 100 homes wrong. That’s pretty solid.
Precision and Recall – These come from the classification report. Precision tells you when the model says a house is “Before 1980”, how often is it actually right? Recall tells you, out of all the houses that really were built before 1980, how many did the model actually catch? The F1-score combines both into one number, which is nice when you want a quick summary.
ROC-AUC – This one was new to me. It basically measures how good the model is at telling the two groups apart. A score of 1.0 means perfect, and 0.5 means the model is just guessing randomly. My model scored around 0.98, which means it’s doing a really good job separating pre-1980 homes from newer ones.

To figure out which features actually matter, I used permutation importance. The idea is if you randomly shuffle a column and the model gets a lot worse, that column must be important. If nothing changes, it probably isn’t.
The top features turned out to be livearea, sprice, and arcstyle_ONE-STORY, which are the same three I guessed would be important back in Task 1, so that was cool to see confirmed. title: Client Report - Baseball Relationships
text:
QUESTION 1
Identify the top 10 players with the longest Major League Baseball careers.
To figure out how long each player’s career was, I used the appearances table since it has a row for every season a player showed up. I grouped by playerID and found the earliest and latest year they played, then just subtracted to get the career length. I also added 1 so that a player who only played in one year still shows up as 1 instead of 0. Then I joined in the people table to get their actual names.
Looking at the results, it’s cool to see how long some of these guys played. A lot of the top careers belong to pitchers, which I didn’t really expect. Nolan Ryan had the longest career in the data at 27 seasons, which is pretty incredible.


Show the code
people = pl.read_database(
    "SELECT playerID, nameFirst, nameLast FROM people",
    con
)

appearances = pl.read_database(
    "SELECT playerID, yearID FROM appearances",
    con
)

top10_careers = (
    appearances
    .group_by("playerID")
    .agg([
        pl.min("yearID").alias("start_year"),
        pl.max("yearID").alias("end_year"),
    ])
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("end_year") - pl.col("start_year") + 1).alias("career_length")
    )
    .join(people, on="playerID", how="left")
    .rename({"nameFirst": "first_name", "nameLast": "last_name"})
    .select(["playerID", "first_name", "last_name", "career_length"])
    .sort("career_length", descending=True)
    .head(10)
)

title: Client Report - Baseball Relationships
text:
QUESTION 2
Show the average salary for each position, including total players and highest salary. Label salaries as High, Medium, or Low.
How I handled players at multiple positions in a single year:
One tricky part was that some players play more than one position in the same season, but they only have one salary. I didn’t want to count that salary multiple times or just pick randomly, so I decided to go with whichever position they played the most games at. That way the salary gets counted toward the position they spent the most time at, which feels like the most fair way to do it. I sorted the fielding table by games played and then grabbed the first row for each player-year to get their main position.


Show the code
fielding = pl.read_database(
    "SELECT playerID, yearID, POS, G FROM fielding",
    con
)

salaries = pl.read_database(
    "SELECT playerID, yearID, salary FROM salaries",
    con
)

# Keep the position with the most games played for each player-year
primary_position = (
    fielding
    .sort(["playerID", "yearID", "G"], descending=[False, False, True])
    .unique(subset=["playerID", "yearID"], keep="first")
    .select(["playerID", "yearID", "POS"])
)

# Join salaries to primary position
salary_by_pos = (
    salaries
    .join(primary_position, on=["playerID", "yearID"], how="left")
    .drop_nulls("POS")
)

# Build summary table
position_summary = (
    salary_by_pos
    .group_by("POS")
    .agg([
        pl.mean("salary").alias("average_salary"),
        pl.n_unique("playerID").alias("total_players"),
        pl.max("salary").alias("highest_salary"),
    ])
    .rename({"POS": "position"})
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("average_salary") > 3_000_000)
        .then(pl.lit("High Salary"))
        .when(pl.col("average_salary") > 2_000_000)
        .then(pl.lit("Medium Salary"))
        .otherwise(pl.lit("Low Salary"))
        .alias("salary_label")
    )
    .sort("average_salary", descending=True)
)

title: Client Report - Missing values
text:
QUESTION 1

Convert values to missing Start with a fresh copy of the flights dataset by reading it in from the link provided above (as opposed to starting with the dataset you ended with in the last assignment). Identify data values that are problematic, non-sense or impossible and change them to be missing. In your report include one record example (one row) from your clean data, in the raw JSON format. Your example should display at least one missing value so that we can verify it was done correctly. (Note: JSON will convert NaN’s to null). Describe your process for finding values that needed to be changed, and how you changed them.

I read the JSON file again from the link and checked each column for bad values.
I changed these values to missing (null):

Bad month names in month (like n/a and Febuary).
Text values in num_of_delays_carrier that are not true numbers (like 1500+).
Negative delay values, because delay counts and delay minutes cannot be negative.

I then printed one cleaned row in raw JSON format that includes at least one null value.


Show the code
import json

# Start from a fresh copy of the source data
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))

# Rule-based cleaning: convert problematic/impossible values to null
valid_months = [
    "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
    "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"
]

df_clean = df.with_columns([
    # Invalid month labels become null
    pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(valid_months))
      .then(pl.col("month"))
      .otherwise(None)
      .alias("month"),

    # Coerce numeric-like strings; invalid strings (for example "1500+") become null
    pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier")
      .cast(pl.Float64, strict=False)
      .cast(pl.Int64, strict=False)
      .alias("num_of_delays_carrier"),

    # Impossible negative values become null
    pl.when(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") < 0)
      .then(None)
      .otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft"))
      .alias("num_of_delays_late_aircraft"),

    pl.when(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas") < 0)
      .then(None)
      .otherwise(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas"))
      .alias("minutes_delayed_nas"),
])

# Quick audit: missing values after cleaning
df_clean.null_count()

# One example cleaned record in raw JSON format with at least one null
row_with_null = df_clean.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal(*[pl.col(c).is_null() for c in df_clean.columns])
).head(1)

example_json = json.dumps(row_with_null.to_dicts()[0], indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
print(example_json)

Show the code
import polars as pl
import requests
import io
import pandas as pd
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
# Load and clean the airport delay data.
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))

airport_lookup = (
  df.filter(pl.col("airport_name") != "")
  .select(["airport_code", "airport_name"])
  .unique(subset=["airport_code"], keep="first")
)

clean = (
  df.filter(pl.col("year").is_not_null())
  .with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("month") == "Febuary")
    .then(pl.lit("February"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("month"))
    .alias("month")
  )
  .join(airport_lookup, on="airport_code", how="left", suffix="_lookup")
  .with_columns(pl.coalesce([pl.col("airport_name_lookup"), pl.col("airport_name")]).alias("airport_name"))
  .drop("airport_name_lookup")
)

airport_year_counts = (
  clean.group_by(["airport_code", "airport_name", "year"])
  .agg(pl.len().alias("sample_size"))
  .sort(["airport_code", "year"])
)

airport_summary = (
  clean.group_by(["airport_code", "airport_name"])
  .agg(
    pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum().alias("total_flights"),
    pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum().alias("total_delayed_flights"),
    (pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum() / pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum()).alias("proportion_delayed"),
    (pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").sum() / pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum() / 60).alias("avg_delay_hours"),
  )
  .sort("proportion_delayed", descending=True)
)

month_summary = (
  clean.filter(pl.col("month") != "n/a")
  .group_by("month")
  .agg(
    pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum().alias("total_flights"),
    pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum().alias("total_delayed_flights"),
    (pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum() / pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum()).alias("proportion_delayed"),
  )
  .sort("proportion_delayed")
)
The dataset needed a little cleaning before I could use it. I removed rows with a missing year, fixed the misspelled month name Febuary, and filled in the blank airport names using the airport code. After that, I had 901 usable rows.

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 1

Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.
I used Vincent and combined Idaho and Utah as an example of summing across multiple state columns. The chart shows the total number of babies with that name in the selected states over time, and the dashed line marks the peak year.


Show the code
# Q1
my_name = "Vincent"
lived_states = ["ID", "UT"]

q1 = (
  df
  .filter(pl.col("name") == my_name)
  .select(
    "year",
    pl.sum_horizontal(*[pl.col(column) for column in lived_states]).alias("lived_states_total")
  )
  .sort("year")
)

q1_peak = q1.sort("lived_states_total", descending=True).head(1)
peak_year = int(q1_peak["year"][0])
peak_count = int(q1_peak["lived_states_total"][0])

ggplot(q1, aes(x="year", y="lived_states_total")) + \
  geom_line(color="#1f4e79", size=1.1) + \
  geom_point(data=q1_peak, mapping=aes(x="year", y="lived_states_total"), color="#d94841", size=3.5) + \
  geom_vline(xintercept=peak_year, linetype="dashed", color="#d94841", size=0.9) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Usage of {my_name} in the Selected States Over Time",
    subtitle=f"States combined: {', '.join(lived_states)}; peak year: {peak_year} ({peak_count})",
    x="Year",
    y="Total Babies"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()

title: Client Report - Unit 1 Task 1: Exploring Names
text:
QUESTION|TASK 4
In the most recent year of data, what was the most common female name in Utah?


Show the code
# names_year is a wide table with no sex-by-year column.
# Estimate female Utah counts using prob_female from names_prob.
most_recent_year = names_year.select(pl.col("year").max()).item()

utah_female_est = (
    names_year
    .filter((pl.col("year") == most_recent_year) & pl.col("UT").is_not_null())
    .select(["name", "UT"])
    .join(
        names_prob.select(["name", "prob_female"]),
        on="name",
        how="left"
    )
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("UT") * pl.col("prob_female").fill_null(0.0)).alias("estimated_female_ut")
    )
    .sort("estimated_female_ut", descending=True)
    .select(["name", "estimated_female_ut"])
    .head(10)
)

top_female_name_ut = utah_female_est.row(0)[0]
top_female_count_ut = utah_female_est.row(0)[1]

print(f"Most recent year in data: {most_recent_year}")
print(f"Top female name in Utah (estimated): {top_female_name_ut} ({top_female_count_ut:,.0f})")
utah_female_est

text:
Show the code
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
df = pl.read_csv("https://posit.byui.edu/content/0d43d66f-d3b7-4c43-8c36-52e3ed69e7f1/names_year_csv.csv")

# Keep only the columns we need for this report.
names = (
  df
  .select(["name", "year", "Total"])
  .with_columns(
    pl.col("name").str.to_titlecase(),
    pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int64),
    pl.col("Total").cast(pl.Int64)
  )
  .rename({"Total": "count"})
)

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.
For this report, I used the name Vincent and birth year 2005. In 2005, Vincent was given 3,737 times, while its historical peak was 6,162 in 1962. That means my birth-year usage is substantial but clearly below the mid-century high, suggesting the name remained popular but had declined from its peak.


Show the code
# Q1
my_name = "Vincent"
birth_year = 2005

q1 = (
  names
  .filter(pl.col("name") == my_name)
  .sort("year")
)

birth_row = q1.filter(pl.col("year") == birth_year)
birth_count = int(birth_row["count"][0]) if birth_row.height > 0 else 0

peak_row = q1.sort("count", descending=True).head(1)
peak_year = int(peak_row["year"][0])
peak_count = int(peak_row["count"][0])

ggplot(q1, aes(x="year", y="count")) + \
  geom_line(color="#1f77b4", size=1.0) + \
  geom_point(data=q1.filter(pl.col("year") == birth_year), color="#d62728", size=3) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Historical U.S. Usage of the Name {my_name}",
    subtitle=f"Birth year ({birth_year}) count: {birth_count}; all-time peak: {peak_count} in {peak_year}",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 2
Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?
I chose Anakin from Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace (1999). The chart shows very low usage before 1999, followed by a clear jump and a higher sustained level afterward. This timing strongly suggests the movie release affected how often the name was chosen.


Show the code
# Q2
movie_name = "Anakin"
release_year = 1999

q2 = (
  names
  .filter(pl.col("name") == movie_name)
  .sort("year")
)

label_df = pl.DataFrame({
  "x": [release_year + 2],
  "y": [int(q2["count"].max() * 0.85)],
  "label": ["Movie release (1999)"]
})

ggplot(q2, aes(x="year", y="count")) + \
  geom_line(color="#2ca02c", size=1.1) + \
  geom_vline(xintercept=release_year, linetype="dashed", color="#d62728", size=1) + \
  geom_text(
    data=label_df,
    mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", label="label"),
    color="#d62728",
    size=10
  ) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Usage of the Name {movie_name} Over Time",
    subtitle="Reference mark shows the movie release year",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 3
Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?
From 1920 to 2000, Mary starts far above the other three names and then drops sharply over time. Martha, Peter, and Paul also decline overall, but with smaller counts and less dramatic slopes than Mary. The main trend is broad long-run decline across all four names, with Mary showing the biggest historical shift.


Show the code
# Q3
target_names = ["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"]

q3 = (
  names
  .filter(
    pl.col("name").is_in(target_names),
    pl.col("year") >= 1920,
    pl.col("year") <= 2000
  )
  .sort(["name", "year"])
)

ggplot(q3, aes(x="year", y="count", color="name")) + \
  geom_line(size=1) + \
  labs(
    title="Christian Name Usage in the U.S. (1920-2000)",
    subtitle="Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul",
    x="Year",
    y="Number of Babies Given Name",
    color="Name"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()

QUESTION 3

According to the BTS website, the “Weather” category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the “Weather” category, but are actually included in both the “nas” and “late_aircraft” categories. Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000’s) delayed by weather (both severe and mild). Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:

100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather

30% of all delayed flights in the “late_aircraft” category are due to weather

From April through August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here

flights = flights.with_columns(
    (
        pl.col("num_of_delays_weather")
        + 0.30 * pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft")
        + pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"]))
          .then(0.40 * pl.col("num_of_delays_nas"))
          .otherwise(0.65 * pl.col("num_of_delays_nas"))
    ).alias("weather_total")
)

text:
In this report, I answer 2 simple questions:

Which delay type is worst: Carrier, Security, or Late Aircraft?
How do those 3 delay types compare across all 7 airports?



Show the code
import io
import requests
import polars as pl
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)




Show the code
# Step 1: Load data.
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
raw = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))

# Step 2: Build a simple airport code -> airport name lookup.
airport_lookup = (
    raw
    .filter(pl.col("airport_name") != "")
    .select(["airport_code", "airport_name"])
    .unique(subset=["airport_code"], keep="first")
)

# Step 3: Keep usable rows and fix obvious text issues.
base = raw.filter(pl.col("year").is_not_null())
base = base.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("month") == "Febuary")
    .then(pl.lit("February"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("month"))
    .alias("month")
)

# Step 4: Fill missing airport names from lookup.
base = (
    base
    .join(airport_lookup, on="airport_code", how="left", suffix="_lookup")
    .with_columns(
        pl.coalesce([pl.col("airport_name_lookup"), pl.col("airport_name")]).alias("airport_name")
    )
    .drop("airport_name_lookup")
)

# Step 5: Convert needed columns to numeric and remove month = n/a.
clean = (
    base
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("num_of_flights_total").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_total").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_security").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft").cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
    )
    .filter(pl.col("month") != "n/a")
)

# Keep only rows that have values for this assignment.
reason_cols = [
    "num_of_delays_carrier",
    "num_of_delays_security",
    "num_of_delays_late_aircraft",
]

analysis = clean.filter(
    pl.col("num_of_flights_total").is_not_null()
    & pl.col("num_of_delays_total").is_not_null()
    & pl.all_horizontal([pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in reason_cols])
)

analysis.select([
    "airport_code",
    "airport_name",
    "month",
    "num_of_flights_total",
    "num_of_delays_total",
    *reason_cols,
]).head(5)

title: Client Report - Flights: Delay Causes (Simple Version)
text:
Question 1: Which delay is worst?
I compare each delay type using:

\text{delay share} = \frac{\text{delays for that reason}}{\text{all flights}}



Show the code
# Convert 3 delay columns into one "reason" column and one "delay_count" column.
reason_map = {
    "num_of_delays_carrier": "Carrier",
    "num_of_delays_security": "Security",
    "num_of_delays_late_aircraft": "Late Aircraft",
}

delay_long = (
    analysis
    .unpivot(
        index=["airport_code", "airport_name", "year", "month", "num_of_flights_total"],
        on=reason_cols,
        variable_name="reason_key",
        value_name="delay_count",
    )
    .with_columns(pl.col("reason_key").replace_strict(reason_map).alias("reason"))
    .drop("reason_key")
)

# Build table for the 3 delay types.
total_flights = analysis.select(pl.sum("num_of_flights_total").alias("total")).item()

overall_reason_table = (
    delay_long
    .group_by("reason")
    .agg(pl.sum("delay_count").alias("total_delays"))
    .with_columns((pl.col("total_delays") / total_flights).alias("delay_share"))
    .with_columns(pl.col("delay_share").round(4))
    .sort("delay_share", descending=True)
)

text:
QUESTION

Take time to get to know your data. Based on some initial exploration, identify 3 variables in the dataset you think will have strong predictive ability for determining if a house was built before 1980. Explain/justify the reasoning behind your guesses (for example with plots).

I looked at the data and picked three variables that seemed like they would be good at telling old homes from newer ones:

livearea: Older homes are just smaller. Homes built before 1980 average about 1,290 sq ft, while newer homes are closer to 1,880 sq ft.
arcstyle_ONE-STORY: Most old homes are one-story. Over half of pre-1980 homes are one-story but only about 6% of newer homes are.
sprice: Older homes sold for a lot less. The average sale price for a pre-1980 home was around $246K compared to over $1M for newer homes.



Show the code
era_label = ["Before 1980" if v == 1 else "1980 or Later" for v in dwellings["before1980"].to_list()]

# Plot 1: livearea by era
p1 = (
    ggplot(
        {"livearea": dwellings["livearea"].to_list(), "era": era_label},
        aes(x="era", y="livearea", fill="era")
    )
    + geom_boxplot(outlier_size=1)
    + labs(x="Era", y="Living Area (sq ft)", title="Living Area by Construction Era")
    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

# Plot 2: proportion of one-story homes by era
p2 = (
    ggplot(
        {
            "era": ["Before 1980", "1980 or Later"],
            "pct": [
                dwellings.filter(pl.col("before1980") == 1)["arcstyle_ONE-STORY"].mean(),
                dwellings.filter(pl.col("before1980") == 0)["arcstyle_ONE-STORY"].mean(),
            ],
        },
        aes(x="era", y="pct", fill="era")
    )
    + geom_bar(stat="identity")
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 1])
    + labs(x="Era", y="Proportion One-Story", title="One-Story Homes by Construction Era")
    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

# Plot 3: sale price by era
p3 = (
    ggplot(
        {"sprice": dwellings["sprice"].to_list(), "era": era_label},
        aes(x="era", y="sprice", fill="era")

    + geom_boxplot(outlier_size=1)

    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

gggrid([p1, p2, p3], ncol=3)



   
       
       
       
   
   
          
   
   




Build a classification model labeling houses as being built “before 1980” or “during or after 1980”. Your goal is to reach or exceed 90% accuracy. Report your final model choice and any other model parameters you may have tweaked (train-test split ratio, tuning parameters, etc).

I built a Random Forest model using a 70/30 train/test split. I left out parcel since it’s just a house ID, yrbuilt because the answer column comes straight from it, and before1980 since that’s what we’re trying to predict.
The model got 92.93% accuracy on the test data, which is above the 90% goal.


Show the code
features = [c for c in dwellings.columns if c not in ("parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980")]

X = dwellings.select(features).to_numpy()
y = dwellings["before1980"].to_numpy()

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42
)

clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)

accuracy = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f} ({accuracy * 100:.2f}%)\n")
print(metrics.classification_report(
    y_test, y_pred, target_names=["1980 or Later", "Before 1980"]
))

title: Client Report - Show me!
text:
QUESTION 1

Repeat the classification exercise using 2 different algorithms/models. Explain the differences between the 3 models (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task).

I ran three different models to see which one is best at guessing whether a Denver home was built before 1980:

Random Forest (original) – This one builds a whole bunch of decision trees (200 of them) and lets them vote on the answer. Because you’re averaging a ton of trees, it doesn’t overfit much and tends to be really reliable. The only downside is it’s kind of a black box, you can’t easily explain exactly why it made a specific call.
Decision Tree (new) – This is basically just one tree that keeps asking yes/no questions about the features until it lands on a prediction. It’s super easy to follow and explain, but one tree on its own tends to be less accurate than the ensemble methods above it.
Gradient Boosting (new) – This one also builds a bunch of trees, but instead of doing it all at once, it builds them one at a time where each new tree tries to fix the mistakes the last one made. It’s usually the most accurate of the three, but it takes the longest to train.



Show the code
# Accuracy summary for quick comparison
for name, y_pred, y_prob in [
    ("Random Forest",      y_pred_rf, y_prob_rf),
    ("Decision Tree",      y_pred_dt, y_prob_dt),
    ("Gradient Boosting",  y_pred_gb, y_prob_gb),
]:
    acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    auc = metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    print(f"{name:<22}  Accuracy: {acc:.4f}   ROC-AUC: {auc:.4f}")


Random Forest           Accuracy: 0.9293   ROC-AUC: 0.9774
Decision Tree           Accuracy: 0.9033   ROC-AUC: 0.9460
Gradient Boosting       Accuracy: 0.9321   ROC-AUC: 0.9787

title: Client Report - Show me!

QUESTION 2

For each model (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task), display the classification report and the permutation importances for the most important features. Discuss which model you would recommend to the Client and why.

All three models do a solid job, but there are some real differences once you look at the numbers:

Random Forest has great precision and recall and the highest ROC-AUC (around 0.98). The features that mattered most were livearea, sprice, and arcstyle_ONE-STORY, which honestly makes a lot of sense since old homes are smaller, cheaper, and often ranch-style.
Decision Tree scores a bit lower across the board, but it picks the same important features, which is a good sign that those features are genuinely useful and not just random noise.
Gradient Boosting is right there with the Random Forest and maybe edges it out slightly, but it takes noticeably longer to train.

My pick for the client: Random Forest. It’s almost as accurate as Gradient Boosting, but it runs faster and is easier to update when new data comes in. The Decision Tree would be great for explaining the general idea to someone non-technical, but I wouldn’t trust it alone for actual predictions.

text:
Question 1 — Load the Star Wars Data
So the first thing I noticed is that this CSV file is a mess straight out of the box. There are 10 rows of random metadata at the top before the actual data even starts, and to make things weirder, the column headers are spread across two separate rows (rows 11 and 12). Polars can’t read this normally, so I had to do a manual cleanup first.
Here’s what I did:

Read row 11 (the first header row) and used regex to trim down the long survey question text into short, usable names and stored it as series a.
Read row 12 (the second header row) and cleaned that up too and stored as series b.
Glued a + b together to make the final column names, then read the actual data starting at row 13.



Show the code
# Step 1 — Read and clean the first header row 
a = pl.Series(
    pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding="utf8-lossy").row(0)
)

# Overwrite the ambiguous "seen any" entry

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
section: Question 2 — Extract Names from the Email Column
text:
Question 2 — Extract Names from the Email Column
This one was actually pretty cool once I figured out regex. The emails have people’s names baked into them (like john.smith@gmail.com or jane_doe@yahoo.com), just separated by a . or _ before the @. So I used three regex patterns to pick out the first name, last name, and domain from each email. It’s basically just telling Python “grab everything before the separator, everything between the separator and the @, and everything after the @.”


Show the code
# Q2: Extract first name, last name, and email domain using regex
df_names = df.select([
    pl.col("email"),
    # First name: letters before the first separator (., _, or digit)
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)(?:[._]|\d)", 1).alias("first_name"),
    # Last name: letters after separator (., _, or digits), before @
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"(?:[._]|\d+)([a-zA-Z]+)@", 1).alias("last_name"),
    # Domain: everything after @, stripped of whitespace
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@(.+)$", 1).str.strip_chars().alias("email_domain"),
])

text:
Question 3 — Domain Count Table
For this one I just needed to count how many people used each email domain. One small gotcha was that some domains were capitalized inconsistently (like Gmail.com vs gmail.com), so I converted everything to lowercase first before grouping. That way they all get counted together instead of as separate domains.


Show the code
# Q3: Count respondents per email domain (case-insensitive)
domain_counts = (
    df.select(
        pl.col("email")
          .str.extract(r"@(.+)$", 1)
          .str.strip_chars()
          .str.to_lowercase()
          .alias("email_domain")
    )
    .group_by("email_domain")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("count"))
    .sort("count", descending=True)
)

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
section: Question 2: Recode Range Variables for Machine Learning
text:
Question 2: Recode Range Variables for Machine Learning
This is the main thing this task is about. Machine learning models can’t do anything with strings like "30-44" or "$50,000 - $99,999". Those need to become actual numbers. So I’m going through each of the problem columns and converting them. For ranges I’m using the midpoint of the range as the number (so "18-29" becomes 23.5), and for ordered categories like education or favorability I’m using ordinal encoding where each level gets a number in order. After making the new numeric column I delete the old string one since we don’t need it anymore.


2a: Age
There are only four age buckets so this one’s pretty simple. I just picked the midpoint for each range. The last one "> 60" is open-ended so there’s no real midpoint, so I went with 65 which seemed like a fair middle-of-the-road guess for that group.


Show the code
age_map = {
    "18-29": 23.5,
    "30-44": 37.0,
    "45-60": 52.5,
    "> 60":  65.0,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("age")
      .replace(age_map, return_dtype=pl.Float64)
      .alias("age_num")
).drop("age")

print("age_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["age_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


age_num unique values: [23.5, 37.0, 52.5, 65.0]





2b: Education
Education is a natural ladder from least to most schooling, so I’m just numbering the levels 1 through 5. No diploma at the bottom gets a 1, grad degree at the top gets a 5. Simple enough.


Show the code
edu_map = {
    "Less than high school degree":       1,
    "High school degree":                 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree":   3,
    "Bachelor degree":                    4,
    "Graduate degree":                    5,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("education")
      .replace(edu_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("education_num")
).drop("education")

print("education_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["education_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


education_num unique values: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]





2c: Household Income
Same idea as age, just use the midpoint of each bracket. The tricky one is "$150,000+" since it has no upper end. I went with $175,000 as a rough estimate. It’s not perfect but it’s better than leaving it as a string or just using 150,000 which would underrepresent that group.


Show the code
income_map = {
    "$0 - $24,999":          12_500,
    "$25,000 - $49,999":     37_500,
    "$50,000 - $99,999":     75_000,
    "$100,000 - $149,999":  125_000,
    "$150,000+":            175_000,
}

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns(
    pl.col("household_income")
      .replace(income_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32)
      .alias("income_num")
).drop("household_income")

print("income_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["income_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))


income_num unique values: [12500, 37500, 75000, 125000, 175000]





2d: Favorability Ratings
This one was actually kind of satisfying to figure out. There are 14 favorability columns (one per character), and they ALL use the exact same 6-level scale, so I don’t have to write the same map 14 times. I can just loop over all the columns that start with fav_ and apply the same replacement to each one in a single pass.
I set "Unfamiliar (N/A)" to 0 instead of null because “I don’t know who this is” is meaningfully different from hating someone. It seemed wrong to just drop those responses.


Show the code
fav_map = {
    "Unfamiliar (N/A)":                             0,
    "Very unfavorably":                             1,
    "Somewhat unfavorably":                         2,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)":  3,
    "Somewhat favorably":                           4,
    "Very favorably":                               5,
}

fav_cols = [c for c in df_seen.columns if c.startswith("fav_")]

df_seen = df_seen.with_columns([
    pl.col(c).replace(fav_map, return_dtype=pl.Int32).alias(c + "_num")
    for c in fav_cols
]).drop(fav_cols)

print(f"Replaced {len(fav_cols)} fav_ columns.")
print("Sample fav_num unique values:", sorted(df_seen["fav_Han_Solo_num"].drop_nulls().unique().to_list()))

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
text:
Executive Summary

Goal: The goal of this project is to clean up the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey data, verify it matches the original article, and build a machine learning model that predicts whether someone makes at least $50k a year.
Data: About 1,186 people were surveyed in 2014 about Star Wars. The dataset includes which movies they’ve seen, how they ranked them, opinions on characters, and some basic demographic info. Fake names and emails were added to the dataset for this project.
Data Prep: I dropped the ID, name, and email columns since they aren’t useful for modeling. I created a yes/no target column from the income column (1 = $50k+, 0 = less than $50k). All text columns were one-hot encoded and missing values were filled in.
Validation: I remade two charts from the original article to check that the dataset looks right.
Model: A Random Forest model hit above 55% accuracy on the test set.

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
text:
Methods
To make sure our data is correct, I recreated two charts from the original FiveThirtyEight article. If they look similar, we can trust the data.
Chart 1 — Films seen (among Star Wars fans)

    "film_key": seen_cols,

    (pl.col("n_seen") / n_fans * 100).alias("pct_seen"),
    pl.col("film_key")
      .str.replace("seen_", "")

# ── Step 1: Identify and drop non-feature columns ──────────────────────────
drop_cols = [
    c for c in df.columns
    if any(kw in c.lower() for kw in ["respondentid", "first_name", "last_name", "email"])
]
print("Columns dropped (ID / name / email):", drop_cols)


Columns dropped (ID / name / email): ['respondentid', 'email']




Show the code
# ── Step 2: Create binary target from household_income ─────────────────────
income_50k_vals = ["$50,000 - $99,999", "$100,000 - $149,999", "$150,000+"]

df_ml = (
    df.drop(drop_cols)
      .filter(pl.col("household_income").is_not_null())   # drop rows with no income label
      .with_columns(
          pl.when(pl.col("household_income").is_in(income_50k_vals))
            .then(pl.lit(1))
            .otherwise(pl.lit(0))
            .alias("target")
      )
)

print("Target distribution:")

# would make the prediction trivial, so we remove it.

# Identify column types for the ColumnTransformer
numeric_cols = feature_df.select(cs.numeric()).columns

# ── Step 4: Convert to pandas (via dict — no pyarrow required) 
# sklearn requires pandas DataFrames or numpy arrays; pyarrow is not installed,
# so we use a dict-of-lists conversion which handles nulls as None → NaN.
X = pd.DataFrame({col: feature_df[col].to_list() for col in feature_df.columns})

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
text:
Results


Show the code
# ── Build preprocessing + classification pipeline
numeric_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))
])

categorical_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_cols),
    ("cat", categorical_transformer, cat_cols)
])

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42))
])




Show the code
# ── Train / test split and model fitting ───────────────────────────────────
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, target, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=target
)

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipeline.predict(X_test)




Show the code
# ── Accuracy and classification report ────────────────────────────────────
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}  ({accuracy * 100:.1f}%)\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))

weighted avg       0.61      0.63      0.61       172

# ── Feature importance (top 20) ───────────────────────────────────────────
rf_model = pipeline.named_steps["classifier"]
ohe = pipeline.named_steps["preprocessor"].named_transformers_["cat"].named_steps["onehot"]

# Reconstruct feature names after one-hot encoding
cat_feature_names = ohe.get_feature_names_out(cat_cols).tolist()
all_feature_names = list(numeric_cols) + cat_feature_names

importance_df = pl.DataFrame({
    "feature": all_feature_names,
    "importance": rf_model.feature_importances_.tolist()

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey
text:
Conclusion
The Random Forest model got above 55% accuracy, which meets the project goal. The most useful features for predicting income were things like age and education, which makes sense. Star Wars opinions didn’t matter as much, but they did help a little.
A few things to keep in mind though:

The survey is from 2014, so the data is pretty old and income patterns have probably changed.
Some people didn’t answer the income question, so I had to remove those rows. If lower-income people were more likely to skip it, that could skew the model.
The model could probably be improved with more data or by tuning the settings.

Overall this was a good exercise in cleaning messy survey data and building a full ML pipeline from scratch.

title: Client Report - Star Wars Survey (Task 1)
section: Question 2 — Extract Names from the Email Column
text:
Question 2 — Extract Names from the Email Column
This one was actually pretty cool once I figured out regex. The emails have people’s names baked into them (like john.smith@gmail.com or jane_doe@yahoo.com), just separated by a . or _ before the @. So I used three regex patterns to pick out the first name, last name, and domain from each email. It’s basically just telling Python “grab everything before the separator, everything between the separator and the @, and everything after the @.”


Show the code
# Q2: Extract first name, last name, and email domain using regex
df_names = df.select([
    pl.col("email"),
    # First name: letters before the first separator (., _, or digit)
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)(?:[._]|\d)", 1).alias("first_name"),
    # Last name: letters after separator (., _, or digits), before @
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"(?:[._]|\d+)([a-zA-Z]+)@", 1).alias("last_name"),
    # Domain: everything after @, stripped of whitespace
    pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@(.+)$", 1).str.strip_chars().alias("email_domain"),
])

For this one I just needed to count how many people used each email domain. title: Star Wars Survey Analysis
text:
Executive Summary

Goal: The goal of this project is to clean up the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey data, verify it matches the original article, and build a machine learning model that predicts whether someone makes at least $50k a year.
Data: About 1,186 people were surveyed in 2014 about Star Wars. The dataset includes which movies they’ve seen, how they ranked them, opinions on characters, and some basic demographic info. Fake names and emails were added to the dataset for this project.
Data Prep: I dropped the ID, name, and email columns since they aren’t useful for modeling. I created a yes/no target column from the income column (1 = $50k+, 0 = less than $50k). All text columns were one-hot encoded and missing values were filled in.
Validation: I remade two charts from the original article to check that the dataset looks right.
Model: A Random Forest model hit above 55% accuracy on the test set.

title: Star Wars Survey Analysis

# ── Build preprocessing + classification pipeline
numeric_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))
])

categorical_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_cols),
    ("cat", categorical_transformer, cat_cols)
])

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42))
])




Show the code
# ── Train / test split and model fitting ───────────────────────────────────
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, target, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=target
)

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipeline.predict(X_test)

print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))

}).sort("importance", descending=True).head(20)

title: Star Wars Survey Analysis
text:
Conclusion
The Random Forest model got above 55% accuracy, which meets the project goal. The most useful features for predicting income were things like age and education, which makes sense. Star Wars opinions didn’t matter as much, but they did help a little.
A few things to keep in mind though:

The survey is from 2014, so the data is pretty old and income patterns have probably changed.
Some people didn’t answer the income question, so I had to remove those rows. If lower-income people were more likely to skip it, that could skew the model.
The model could probably be improved with more data or by tuning the settings.

Overall this was a good exercise in cleaning messy survey data and building a full ML pipeline from scratch.
